coredata.dc:title: A novel sandwich-like cytosensor based on aptamers-modified magnetic beads and carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets for circulating tumor cells detection
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Chenlan
Zhong, Liang
Xiong, Ling
Liu, Chen
Yu, Lihua
Chu, Xuan
Luo, Xu
Zhao, Min
Liu, Beizhong
coredata.dc:description: As a part of "tumor fluid biopsy", circulating tumor cells (CTCs) contain enormous tumor information, so the detection of CTCs is very helpful in clinical diagnosis and therapy. Here, we applied aptamers-modified magnetic beads (Apt@MBs) and carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets systems (CDs/CoOOH) to develop a new cytosensor for ultrasensitive and specific detection of CTCs. At first, amino-modified magnetic beads (MBs) were cross-linked with carboxyl-modified MUC1 aptamers (Apt) to gain Apt@MBs. Then, folic acid (FA) functionalized carbon dots (FA@CDs) were assembled onto cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH) to obtain FA@CDs/CoOOH with fluorescence quenching. In the detection systems, Apt@MBs identified and accumulated MCF-7 cells, forming Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells, which then combined with FA@CDs/CoOOH to obtain sandwich-like Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH. Finally, ascorbic acid (AA) decomposed CoOOH to release carbon dots (CDs), and the fluorescence of abundant CDs act as efficient signal output. The cytosensor detected MCF-7 cells with a wide dynamic range (10 ∼ 105 cells/mL) and a low limit of detection (5 cells/mL), owing to the specific identification and effective enrichment of Apt@MBs as well as anti-interference capability and signal amplification of FA@CDs/CoOOH. Most importantly, the cytosensor successfully discriminated MCF-7 cells from white blood cells, exhibiting potentiality in ultrasensitive CTCs detection in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs)
Magnetic separation
Carbon dots (CDs)
Cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH)
Sandwich-Like cytosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of folic acid-modified carbon dots (FA@CDs)
Target recognition ability of Apt and FA@CDs to MCF-7 cells
Feasibility verification of the developed fluorescence cytosensor in MCF-7 cells detection
Performance analysis of the developed fluorescence cytosensor
Applicability of the developed cytosensor in MCF-7 cells detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (A) CDs (inset: the size distribution of CDs) and (B) CDs/CoOOH (inset: the size distribution of CDs in CDs/CoOOH). (C) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission spectra (b) of CDs (50 μg/mL) and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH (0.55 mg/mL) (c). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH (0.55 mg/mL) (a), CDs (50 μg/mL) (b), CDs/CoOOH (c) and CDs/CoOOH + AA (500 μM) (d). (E) Photos of CoOOH (a; a’), CDs (b; b’), CoOOH/CDs (c; c’) and CoOOH/CDs + AA (d; d’) under natural light (up) and UV light (down). (F) Fluorescence signal of CDs (50 μg/mL) (a), CDs/CoOOH (b) and CDs/CoOOH with different concentration of AA (from c to g: 400 μM, 800 μM, 2.4 mM, 4 mM and 8 mM).

Fluorescence responses of the cytosensor without cells (the blank control, Column a), with HEK293 cells (the negative control, Column b), with MCF-7 cells but without AA (Column c) and with MCF-7 cells and AA (the positive control, Column d).

Changes of fluorescence signal induced by variations of the concentration of Apt in Apt@MBs manufacture (A), the ratio of FA@CDs to CoOOH in FA@CDs/CoOOH manufacture (B), the dosage of Apt@MBs in detection systems (C), the dosage of AA (D), the time for MCF-7 cells (E) and FA@CDs/CoOOH (F) incubation (Others were in their optimal state when one of the parameters changed).

(A) Fluorescence signal obtained by MCF-7 cells of different concentrations (a to g: 10, 102, 103, 5 × 103, 104, 5 × 104 and 105 cells/mL), and (B) Calibration curve of fluorescence assays. (C) Fluorescence signal in response to different cells (a: Blank; b: HEK293; c: HEGP2; d: A549; e: MCF-7; f: HEK293 + HEGP2 + A549 + MCF-7). The standard deviation of three independent experiments resulted in the error bars.

Fluorescence responses obtained in WBC (105 cells/mL) samples containing increasing MCF-7 cells (from a to g: 50, 102, 103, 5 × 103, 104, 5 × 104 and 105 cells/mL) (A). Calibration curve for fluorescence assays of MCF-7 cells in WBC samples (B). Error bars were calculated based on three independent experiments.

Schematic diagram of sandwich-like fluorescence cytosensor based on aptamers-modified magnetic beads and carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets for ultrasensitive and specific CTCs detection. (A) The preparation process of FA@CDs/CoOOH, the quenching of CDs fluorescence by CoOOH and the recovery of CDs fluorescence through reduction of CoOOH by AA. (B) The detection process of CTCs by the developed sandwich-like fluorescence cytosensor.

head.ce:title: A novel sandwich-like cytosensor based on aptamers-modified magnetic beads and carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets for circulating tumor cells detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Laboratory of Yongchuan Hospital, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 402160, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clinical Laboratory of Yongchuan Hospital, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 402160, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education), College of Laboratory Medicine, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Clinical Laboratory Diagnostics (Ministry of Education)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a part of "tumor fluid biopsy", circulating tumor cells (CTCs) contain enormous tumor information, so the detection of CTCs is very helpful in clinical diagnosis and therapy. Here, we applied aptamers-modified magnetic beads (Apt@MBs) and carbon dots/cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets systems (CDs/CoOOH) to develop a new cytosensor for ultrasensitive and specific detection of CTCs. At first, amino-modified magnetic beads (MBs) were cross-linked with carboxyl-modified MUC1 aptamers (Apt) to gain Apt@MBs. Then, folic acid (FA) functionalized carbon dots (FA@CDs) were assembled onto cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH) to obtain FA@CDs/CoOOH with fluorescence quenching. In the detection systems, Apt@MBs identified and accumulated MCF-7 cells, forming Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells, which then combined with FA@CDs/CoOOH to obtain sandwich-like Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH. Finally, ascorbic acid (AA) decomposed CoOOH to release carbon dots (CDs), and the fluorescence of abundant CDs act as efficient signal output. The cytosensor detected MCF-7 cells with a wide dynamic range (10 ∼ 105 cells/mL) and a low limit of detection (5 cells/mL), owing to the specific identification and effective enrichment of Apt@MBs as well as anti-interference capability and signal amplification of FA@CDs/CoOOH. Most importantly, the cytosensor successfully discriminated MCF-7 cells from white blood cells, exhibiting potentiality in ultrasensitive CTCs detection in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: The dissemination of tumor cells from the primary tumor site into body fluids is an early event in the progression of some advanced cancer. Among them, tumor cells disseminating into the blood were called circulating tumor cells (CTCs) [1,2]. As a role of "tumor fluid biopsy", CTCs are regarded as more available and vitally critical biomarkers in early detection, metastasis and recurrence of tumors [3]. Therefore, the detection of CTCs is of important clinical significance. However, it’s too challengeable to develop CTCs analysis for their extreme rarity in complex matrix [4]. To achieve accurate analysis, CTCs enrichment is commonly required utilizing the properties of size, charge, density, or other biological properties [5,6]. Currently, microfluidic chips and magnetic beads-based separation and enrichment assays are main approaches [7,8]. Compared with the complex manufacture and high cost of microfluidic chips, magnetic beads have received increasing attention because of their advantages of simple synthesis, low cost, strong stability and rapid separation [9–12]. Aptamers, a class of artificial oligonucleotides selected by systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment, can specifically combine with various targets, which have wide application prospects as recognition elements in clinical diagnosis for their excellent programmability, easy modification, high stability and cost-effective production [13–17]. By combining magnetic beads and aptamers, the aptamers-modified magnetic beads are prepared as an effective separation and enrichment tool, which are beneficial for the capture of CTCs.

After the separation and enrichment of CTCs from blood, sensitive signal output is another critical step for the accurate determination of CTCs. In clinical laboratory, fluorescence methods (e.g., histochemical staining, immunohistochemistry and flow cytometry) have been applied in detecting tumor cells due to their intrinsic advantages of accurate controllability, easy operation and high sensitivity [18–22]. In these methods, the sensitivity of histochemical staining is not sufficient for accurate identification of tumor cells with low abundance due to various interferences during detection [18]. Besides, immunohistochemistry, as the most commonly used direct detection method for cancer pathological diagnosis, is complex in analyzing body fluid specimens due to the time-consuming processes of cytopathological slides preparation, fixation, antigen retrieval and so on [19,20]. As for flow cytometry, although it can achieve sensitivity of analysis, it still has problems such as antibody inactivation, costly reagents and instruments [21,22]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop an efficient and pragmatic fluorescence cytosensor for CTCs detection with high analytical sensitivity and specificity.

Carbon dots (CDs) with many merits, such as easy and rapid synthesis, aqueous solubility, high quantum yield and low toxicity, are considered as a promising fluorescence probe in bioanalysis [23,24]. Traditionally, CDs are labelled with recognition elements to be the signal labels for fluorescence analysis [25,26]. However, this way shows limited sensitivity because of the low signal to noise ratio. Delightedly, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials-based quenchers are introduced to immobilize the signal labels for fluorescence signal quenching. Then, the target recognition triggers the release of signal labels from the 2D nanomaterials for fluorescence signal recovery. This signal switching mode has significantly increased the signal to noise ratio [27–29]. However, the target recognition-induced signal recovery is not applicable for the low abundance targets analysis. Whether we can decompose quenching 2D nanomaterials to realize the signal recovery of signal labels? Lately, cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets (CoOOH), as a new layered transition metal 2D nanomaterial with simple synthesis, can be served as broad-spectrum quenchers through surface energy transfer (SET) based on its broad and strong absorption band at 410 nm or so [30]. Meanwhile, CoOOH-based strategy can conquer the susceptible repeatability of previous fluorescence methods depending on the aggregation and dispersion of quenchers [31,32]. Although, based on the characteristic that CoOOH can be decomposed into Co2+ by AA effectively and selectively, CoOOH-based fluorescence strategies have been employed in the monitoring nucleic acids and proteins, as well as imaging AA intracellular and even in vivo, the exploration of CoOOH for bio/chemical assay is still at early stage [33–38]. Inspired by the above-mentioned superior nature of CoOOH, the CDs/CoOOH systems is developed. Then, AA is used to reduce CoOOH to release CDs for signal recovery, which is a novel fluorescence signal amplification strategy to improve the detection sensitivity.

Herein, an ultrasensitive and specific fluorescence cytosensor for MCF-7 cells detection was innovatively constructed by combing Apt@MBs as the separation and enrichment tool while CDs/CoOOH as signal systems. As shown in Scheme 1 A, FA was cross-link onto CDs, which was further assembled onto the CoOOH to obtain the fluorescence quenching FA@CDs/CoOOH. Then, after adding AA, the CoOOH would be reduced and the fluorescence of CDs would recover efficiently. As exhibited in Scheme 1B, Apt were anchored onto MBs to prepare the Apt@MBs, which enabled specific capture and enrichment of MCF-7 cells over-expressing MUC1 in the complex matrix. In the detection process, the MCF-7 cells, as target cells, would be captured by Apt@MBs and enriched through an external magnetic field. Next, FA@CDs/CoOOH could also specifically bound to MCF-7 cells via the affinity between FA and folate receptor (FR) over-expressing on the cancer cells. As a consequence, the sandwich-like cytosensor (Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH) was obtained with the quenching fluorescence state. Then, the effective reduction of CoOOH could recover the fluorescence of CDs efficiently with the addition of AA. It was worth noting that Apt@MBs could not only effectively separate and enrich target cells, but also prevented the output of the fluorescence signal from interference in the system when they were with CDs/CoOOH in the innovative combination. Finally, the MCF-7 cells were detected due to significant difference in fluorescence response with considerable specificity and sensitivity. The developed method possessed a certain potential for the clinical diagnosis and scientific research of rare disseminated CTCs in complex matrix.

All the cell lines (MCF-7, HEK293, HEGP2 and A549) were from the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) provided all the HPLC-purified oligonucleotides (MUC1 aptamer: 5'-COOH-GCAGTTGATCCTTTGGATACCCTGG-3'; MUC1 aptamer-FAM: 5'-FAM-GCAGTTGATCCTTTGGATACCCTGG-3'), 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid hydrate (MES), citric acid (CA) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH). Amino-modified magnetic beads (MBs) with the particle size of 200 nm (10 mg/mL) were from Jingpan biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cobaltous chloride (CoCl2), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimidehydrochloride (EDC) were got from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co., Ltd (MO, USA). Sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) was provided by Chron chemicals Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Folic acid (FA) and polyethylenimine (PEI) were from Adamas-beta reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA) was obtained from Huada Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China).

MES buffer (0.1 M; pH 6.0) and PBS buffer (10 mM; containing: 2 mM KH2PO4, 2.7 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, 137 mM NaCl; PH 7.4) were made of ultra-pure Milli-Q water provided by a Millipore water purification system (MA, USA). All the reagents used in experiments were of analytical grade.

The fluorescence signal was obtained with a Synergy™ H1 Hybrid multimode microplate reader (BioTek, USA). The concentrations of DNA suspensions were tested by a NanoDrop 1000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The colourimetry signal was monitored by a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were employed to characterize nanomaterials (e.g., CoOOH, CDs and CDs/CoOOH) by a Tecnai G2 F20 (200KV) (FEI, USA). A FC-500 Flow Cytometer from Beckman Coulter (USA) was used for the examination of fluorescence labelled cells.

Apt was attached to the MBs by the covalent cross-link of EDC/NHS. At first, 60 μL 50 mM EDC and 20 μL 10 μM Apt were mixed with 450 μL MES buffer for 30 min to get activated-carboxyl Apt. Then, 60 μL 50 mM NHS was added in and stirred for additional 30 min. Subsequently, 10 μL 10 mg/mL MBs were introduced into the system with continuous stir for another 48 h. Afterwards, PBS buffer was used to wash the Apt@MBs with a magnetic field to remove excessive Apt. Finally, the obtained Apt@MBs were expanded capacity to 400 μL with PBS buffer and kept at 4 °C for later use.

According to the reported literature, CoOOH was prepared with minor modification [32]. Firstly, 300 μL 1.0 M NaOH and 1.0 mL 10 mM CoCl2 were mixed together with 15 min ultrasonic treatment. Next, the precipitate of the above mixture was obtained after a centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, which was then dispersed into 1.0 mL deionized water. The reactant was sonicated for another 20 min after adding 50 μL 0.9 M NaClO. After centrifugation and wash, 1.0 mL deionized water was added in and then stored at 4 °C.

CDs used in this work were synthesized by the published literature [37]. Briefly, 0.4 g PEI and 2.0 g CA were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water with ultrasound treatment for 15 min, and then the above mixture was put into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, when cooled naturally to room temperature, a centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 10 min was carried out to remove large particles of the products. Then, a dialysis bag was used to dialyze the supernatant against deionized water for 24 h. Eventually, the products inside the dialysis bag were CDs with fluorescence, which were lyophilized and then redispersed in deionized water and stored in a brown flask at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of folic acid-modified carbon dots (FA@CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 2.3 mg FA, 1.0 mg EDC and 1.5 mg NHS were dispersed into MES buffer with the total volume of 6 mL, and then stirred continuously for 2 h. After adding 3 mL 50 μg/mL prepared CDs and another stir for 48 h, the products were dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h. At last, the obtained FA@CDs were stored at 4 °C in a brown flask.

The procedures of the FA@CDs/CoOOH were described as follows. Typically, 1.0 mL CoOOH (6.5 mg/mL) and 250 μL prepared FA@CDs were mixed with 1.75 mL deionized water, which were then sonicated for 20 min and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. Eventually, the FA@CDs/CoOOH was acquired and stored in a brown flask at 4 °C.

Initially, MCF-7 cells of various concentrations diluted in PBS buffer with a total volume of 150 μL were made as detection samples in this part, which were then put into 50 μL Apt@MBs suspension and incubated for 90 min. The Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells were obtained by an external magnetic field, owning to the specific binding of Apt@MBs with MUC1 over-expressing on the surface of MCF-7 cells. Afterwards, the resultant Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells were dispersed into PBS buffer with a final volume of 50 μL, and reacted with 150 μL prepared FA@CDs/CoOOH for 45 min. In the influence of FA to FR over-expressing on MCF-7 cells, the sandwich-like Apt@MB/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH were obtained by a magnetic separation. Subsequently, 2 μM AA was added into the Apt@MB/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH and then reacted for 15 min to absolutely reduce CoOOH, releasing CDs for fluorescence recovery. The reaction temperature in this part was 37 °C. After 10 s of oscillating, the fluorescence intensity value at the maximum emission wavelength was recorded, while the gain was set at 100.

The structure and morphology of CDs were showed using HRTEM image. The result in Fig. 1 A clearly showed that the prepared CDs of good homogeneity and dispersity were monodispersed with an average diameter of about 3 nm. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S1, TEM image of CoOOH with uniform hexagonal nanosheets structure could supply a large area to FA@CDs for immobilization. Moreover, the CDs/CoOOH was also viewed by HRTEM image in Fig. 1B. It could be seen that massive CDs (blue arrows) were uniformly distributed on CoOOH (red arrows). Moreover, the insets of Fig. 1A and B showed that the size distribution of CDs in CDs/CoOOH agreed with the size distribution of CDs alone.

Additionally, the optical properties of CDs and CoOOH were investigated by both fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, Curve a depicted the maximum excitation spectrum of CDs was 350 nm, while Curve b showed the maximum emission spectrum of CDs was 450 nm. Moreover, Curve c showed two apparent characteristic absorption peaks of CoOOH were observed at 252 nm and 401 nm. It served to show that the emission spectrum of CDs was overlapped by the characteristic absorption peaks of CoOOH, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs would be quenched by CoOOH through SET [30].

The process of CoOOH reducing by AA to release CDs was verified by UV–vis absorption spectrum. From Curve a in Fig. 1D, we could see that CoOOH had a broad UV–vis absorption band of 200∼800 nm and two maximum absorption peaks at 252 nm and 401 nm. Besides, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs showed that there were two absorption bands revolved around 240 and 359 nm (Curve b), which might be put down to the π-π* transition of CC bond and the uniform distribution of sp2 hybridization in sp3 cluster. However, when CDs were assembled onto the surface of CoOOH to form CDs/CoOOH, the UV–vis absorption band of CoOOH appeared blue-shift (Curve c). After AA being added into the mixture of CDs/CoOOH, the UV–vis absorption band of CoOOH disappeared, accomplished by the appearance of the absorption peak of CDs at 359 nm (Curve d), indicating that AA could reduce CoOOH and then release CDs (the reaction scheme between CoOOH and AA was shown in Fig. S2).

Moreover, the fluorescence quenching ability of CoOOH towards CDs and fluorescence recovery by adding AA were also confirmed. As shown in Fig. 1E, the photographs of different solutions (CoOOH, CDs, CDs/CoOOH and CDs/CoOOH + AA) taken under natural light and UV light were identical with the expectations. Meanwhile, Fig. 1F showed that fluorescence of CDs (a) was efficiently quenched by CoOOH with 98.69 % (b). However, the fluorescence signal recovered according to the increasing addition of AA (c–g). Finally, after adding adequate AA, nearly 90.39 % fluorescence recovery of CDs was obtained (g). These results indicated that CoOOH had effective quenching ability to CDs, which would be deprived by AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target recognition ability of Apt and FA@CDs to MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The flow cytometry analysis was applied to confirm the recognition ability of Apt and FA@CDs to MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence signal of MCF-7 cells incubated with FAM-labeled Apt (b) was 100 times higher than the fluorescence signal of MCF-7 cells alone (a). Moreover, the fluorescence signal of MCF-7 cells reacted with FA@CDs (c) had a ten-fold increase towards that of MCF-7 cells alone (a). These results indicated the strong target recognition capabilities of Apt and FA@CDs to MCF-7 cells, ensuring the successful establishment of sandwich-like cytosensor for MCF-7 cells detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility verification of the developed fluorescence cytosensor in MCF-7 cells detection
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of the fluorescence cytosensor in MCF-7 cells detection, a variety of control experiments were conducted by studying important control parameters of Apt@MBs, the target MCF-7 cells, FA@CDs/CoOOH and AA for cytosensor analysis (Fig. 2 ). As the blank control, the reaction systems containing Apt@MBs, FA@CDs/CoOOH and AA were investigated (Column a). Obviously, negligible fluorescence signal was observed when there were only Apt@MBs, FA@CDs/CoOOH and AA. This result indicated that FA@CDs/CoOOH could not combine Apt@MBs in absence of MCF-7 cells as mediations. Additionally, MUC1- cells HEK293 (105 cells/mL) were used as the negative control. Significantly, if HEK293 cells were added into the above mixture, barely change of the fluorescence signal exhibited relative to Column a, which was on account of the incapacity of Apt@MBs to capture HEK293 cells in the systems (Column b). Meanwhile, the role of AA in the systems was also proved (Column c). When Apt@MBs, FA@CDs/CoOOH and MCF-7 cells coexisted in the systems, the sandwich-like Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH were obtained, because MCF-7 cells were cancer cells over-expressing both MUC1 and FR, which would bond with Apt@MBs as well as FA@CDs/CoOOH simultaneously. However, due to the absence of AA, CoOOH could not be reduced to recover the fluorescence signal of CDs (Column c). As expected, the fluorescence signal increased distinctly relative to other groups when Apt@MBs, MCF-7 cells and FA@CDs/CoOOH coexisted in the systems and the captured Apt@MBs/MCF-7 cells/FA@CDs/CoOOH reduced by AA (Column d). On the one hand, the high detection efficiency of the developed cytosensor owed a good deal to the large surface area of CoOOH to provide lots of binding sites for FA@CDs immobilization, thus generating high fluorescence signal. On the other hand, the fluorescence signal of CDs derived from the supernatant by Apt@MBs could avoid interferences of other participating substances. These results indicated that this fluorescence strategy was of good feasibility in differentiating MCF-7 cells from MUC1- expressing cells efficiently.

Different experimental parameters, including the concentration of Apt in Apt@MBs manufacture, the ratio of FA@CDs to CoOOH in FA@CDs/CoOOH manufacture, the dosage of Apt@MBs in detection systems, the dosage of AA and the time of MCF-7 cells and FA@CDs/CoOOH incubation, were regarded as influencing factors to obtain optimal analytical performance of the developed cytosensor (Fig. 3 ). In this part, the MCF-7 cells of 105 cells/mL were used as samples.

The concentration of Apt in Apt@MBs manufacture had an important influence on capture efficiency of Apt@MBs, so we optimized the concentration of Apt at first. As shown in Fig. 3A, it was clear that the fluorescence signal increased as the augment of Apt from 4 to 10 μM, but there was on significant change after 10 μM. So, 10 μM was adopted as the optimal Apt concentration in Apt@MBs manufacture.

Because the ratio of FA@CDs to CoOOH in FA@CDs/CoOOH manufacture was a significant element for the target binding efficiency and signal systems construction, the ratio between FA@CDs and CoOOH was optimized. The results showed that the fluorescence signal increased when the ratio was from 5:1 to 1:4, then tended to decrease after 1:4 (Fig. 3B). Therefore, the optimal ratio between FA@CDs and CoOOH in FA@CDs/CoOOH manufacture was 1:4.

The dosage of Apt@MBs in detection systems was an important factor for the performance of the developed cytosensor, so the details of the optimization process were discussed. The experimental results (Fig. 3C) showed that the fluorescence signal increased with the dosage of Apt@MBs (0.25 mg/mL) from 5 to 50 μL, but decreased slightly when the dosage further increased from 50 to 90 μL, which might be because that excessive Apt@MBs on MCF-7 cells would affect the combination between MCF-7 cells and FA@CDs/CoOOH, thus reducing the output signal. Therefore, 50 μL was chosen as the best dosage of Apt@MBs in detection systems.

Then, the dosage of AA was another essential element for experiment efficiency. So, the dosage of AA reacting with captured FA@CDs/CoOOH was explored within the scope from 0.1 μM to 3.0 μM. The fluorescence signal increased with the addition of AA from 0.1 μM to 2.0 μM and tended to decrease after 2.0 μM, which suggested that the best dosage of AA in this scheme was 2.0 μM (Fig. 3D). Excessive AA weakening the fluorescence of CDs could be attributed to that excessive AA affected the amino functional groups on the surface of CDs and might cause the change of n-π* electron transition of CO bond and the change of π-π* electron transition of conjugate CC bond, thus affecting the fluorescence of CDs [39,40].

Besides, reaction time also had important effect on experimental efficiency. Herein, the time of MCF-7 cells and FA@CDs/CoOOH incubation was optimized, respectively. It was obvious that with the prolonged time for MCF-7 cells incubation, the fluorescence signal significantly increased until 90 min, then tended to plateau (Fig. 3E). Also, the time range for FA@CDs/CoOOH capture was set at 5 ∼ 75 min. At the beginning, fluorescence signal increased quickly with the additional time, yet no significant change was observed after 45 min (Fig. 3F). Therefore, we could easily choose 90 min as the optimal incubation time for MCF-7 cells while 45 min as the best effective incubation time for FA@CDs/CoOOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the developed fluorescence cytosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the specificity in MCF-7 cells detection of the developed fluorescence cytosensor, the cross-reactivity studies were carried out against interfering MUC1- cells (HEK293, HEGP2 and A549). Fig. 4C showed that despite the high concentrations (106 cells/mL), these interfering MUC1- cells achieved very weak fluorescence signal (Column b, c and d) approximating to the blank (Column a). However, because MCF-7 cells (105 cells/mL) could be analyzed specifically by the developed cytosensor, there was a remarkable fluorescence signal increasing shown in Column e. Then, these interfering MUC1- cells (106 cells/mL) were mixed with MCF-7 cells (105 cells/mL) to observe their influences in MCF-7 cells detection. As expected, the mixture exhibited acceptable deviation compared with the fluorescence signal of MCF-7 cells alone (Column f). These results revealed that these interfering MUC1- cells had little influence on the detection of MCF-7 cells, which manifested the high specificity of the developed cytosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of the developed cytosensor in MCF-7 cells detection

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a novel signal amplification strategy combining Apt@MBs with CDs/CoOOH was designed to detect MCF-7 cells ultrasensitively and specifically. Interestingly, Apt@MBs-based separation and enrichment as well as CDs/CoOOH-based signal amplification strategy of the MCF-7 cells were executed in the homogeneous phase, ensuring the effective recognition reaction. Even more remarkably, CoOOH would quench the fluorescence of CDs dramatically before adding AA to achieve lower background signal. Then, the fluorescence recovery of CDs caused after the decomposition of CoOOH by AA, obtaining the strong signal putout. More importantly, the designed signal mode could not only significantly amplify the fluorescence signal output, but also prevent the interferences from other participants in the system through the combination between Apt@MBs and CDs/CoOOH. In general, the developed cytosensor exhibited high specificity and low detection limit (5 cells/mL) in discrimination of MCF-7 cells, which was of potential clinical application value in diagnosis and treatment of cancers.

Chenlan Shen: Software, Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Investigation, Project administration. Liang Zhong: Software, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Ling Xiong: Methodology, Formal analysis. Chen Liu: Data curation, Conceptualization. Lihua Yu: Resources, Software. Xuan Chu: Resources, Validation. Xu Luo: Software. Min Zhao: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Beizhong Liu: Funding acquisition, Data curation, Supervision, Methodology, Project administration, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The following funds supported this study: the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81772280 and 21804015), the Chongqing Graduate Research Innovation Project (No. CYB19153) and the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (No. cstc2018jcyjAX0206).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129399.

Supplementary data Experimental (cell culture and treatments, verification of Apt and FA@CDs for MCF-7 cells capture). TEM image of CoOOH. The reaction scheme of CoOOH reduction by AA. Fluorescence spectra of CDs and FA@CDs. Characterization of CoOOH and its reaction with AA. Characterization of Apt@MBs and combination analysis of Apt and FA@CDs to MCF-7 cells. Stability of Apt@MBs and FA@CDs/CoOOH. Comparison of this developed method with other aptamers-based cytosensors for MCF-7 cells determination.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenlan Shen is currently a graduate student of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is the development of biosensing strategy for clinical diagnosis.

Liang Zhong is currently a teacher of Chongqing Medical University. Her research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis and treatment targets and the development of new cancer diagnosis and treatment methods.

Ling Xiong is currently a graduate student of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis and treatment targets and the development of new leukemia treatment methods.

Chen Liu is currently a graduate student of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis and treatment targets and the development of new leukemia treatment methods.

Lihua Yu is currently an employee of Yongchuan hospital affiliated of chongqing medical university. Her research interest is research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis and treatment targets and the development of new leukemia diagnosis methods.
Xuan Chu is currently a graduate student of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis.
Xu Luo is currently a graduate student of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis.

Min Zhao is currently a teacher of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. Her research interest is the development of biosensing strategy for clinical laboratory diagnosis.

Beizhong Liu is currently a professor of Chongqing Medical University majoring in clinical laboratory diagnosis. His research interest is the screening of leukemia diagnosis and treatment targets and the development of new cancer diagnosis and treatment methods.
